algorithms.title: Advanced Airway Management During Cardiac Arrest
algorithms.description: Decision pathway for securing an advanced airway during CPR, integrating BVM, supraglottic airways, and ET intubation with capnography confirmation.
steps: Cardiac arrest recognized — begin high-quality CPR with BVM ventilation (one breath every 6 seconds, visible chest rise).
Determine if an advanced airway is indicated and which device to use based on available skill and equipment.
If ET intubation is chosen and an experienced provider is available: perform laryngoscopy (video preferred) during continuous compressions; limit attempt to ≤10 seconds.
If SGA is chosen (or intubation is not immediately feasible): insert i-gel or King LT during continuous compressions (<15 seconds typical).
After device placement, confirm position with waveform capnography.
If waveform is present (characteristic rectangular CO2 waveform): continue CPR with continuous capnography monitoring.
If waveform is absent: assume esophageal placement — remove/replace the device, return to BVM between attempts.
Once advanced airway confirmed: deliver 1 breath every 6 seconds (10 breaths/min) independently of compressions.
Monitor ETCO2 throughout CPR: target ≥10 mmHg for adequate compression quality; sudden sustained rise ≥40 mmHg suggests ROSC.
If ETCO2 drops or waveform disappears: re-check tube/device position, then reassess CPR quality and reversible causes.

algorithms.title: ETCO2-Guided CPR Quality Monitoring
algorithms.description: Using continuous waveform capnography to assess and improve CPR quality during cardiac arrest.
steps: Advanced airway placed and confirmed — continuous waveform capnography active on monitor.
Measure ETCO2 after each cycle of compressions.
If ETCO2 ≥10 mmHg: compressions are likely generating adequate cardiac output — continue current technique.
If ETCO2 persistently <10 mmHg: systematically reassess CPR quality.
Check compression rate: should be 100–120 compressions per minute.
Check compression depth: at least 2 inches (5 cm) in adults, allow full chest recoil.
Check compression fraction: should be ≥80%.
If CPR quality is adequate, evaluate for reversible causes: hypovolemia, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, massive pulmonary embolism, severe acidosis.
If ETCO2 suddenly rises to ≥40 mmHg and remains elevated: suspect ROSC — perform manual pulse check.
If pulse is confirmed: transition to post-cardiac arrest care; if no pulse, continue CPR and reassess.
